verses.commentary: Jesus addresses crowds AND disciples — public denunciation. The audience matters: the rebuke is also a warning to disciples about how not to lead.
Scribes and Pharisees 'sit in Moses' seat' — claim teaching authority over Torah. The role is acknowledged before the abuse is exposed.
Do what they say, not what they do. Their teaching where it aligns with Moses is valid; their lives do not match their teaching. Hypocrisy diagnosed.
They tie up heavy burdens — pile up oral law on others. They themselves will not move them with a finger. Leadership weaponized rather than embodied.
Everything for show. Phylacteries (tefillin, prayer-boxes from Deuteronomy 6:8) made wide; tassels (kraspeda, κράσπεδα; tzitzit) made long. Religious props inflated for visibility.
Love the chief seats at banquets, front seats in synagogues. Status-seeking in religious settings. Honor-economy operates inside Torah-economy.
Greetings in marketplaces, title 'Rabbi' (rabbi, ραββί, my great one). Public deference craved.
'Not so among you. Do not be called Rabbi — you have one teacher, you are all brothers.' Disciple equality. Hierarchy under one master only.
'Call no one on earth your father — you have one Father in heaven.' Honorific titles that claim spiritual paternity are reserved to God.
'Not called instructors (kathegetai, καθηγηταί) — one is your instructor, the Christ.' Threefold prohibition on hierarchical religious titles. The Messiah's community is structurally horizontal.
Greatest = servant. The kingdom inversion repeated (compare 20:26). Greatness denoted by service rendered, not titles received.
Self-exalters humbled, self-humblers exalted. Eschatological reversal compressed into a proverb.
First woe. Shut the kingdom of heaven in people's faces. Pharisees neither enter nor permit entry. Their gatekeeping blocks access.

[Textual variant absent from earliest manuscripts. Later manuscripts add: 'Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! For you devour widows' houses and for a pretense make long prayers; therefore you will receive the greater condemnation.' Scribal harmonization with Mark 12:40 / Luke 20:47.]
Second woe. Travel land and sea to make one proselyte — then make him twice as much a son of Gehenna. Missionary zeal in service of death-doctrine. Successful evangelism for the wrong gospel.
Third woe. Blind guides! Their oath-rules: swearing by temple = nothing, swearing by temple gold = binding. Absurd inversion.
Blind fools! What is greater — gold or the temple that sanctifies it? The container outranks the contents.
Same pattern with altar and gift. Hairsplitting rules that elevate offering over altar.
Altar sanctifies gift; gift does not sanctify altar. Their categories are inverted from sacrificial Torah logic.
Whoever swears by altar swears by everything on it. Their distinctions evaporate under examination.
Swearing by temple swears by the one who dwells (katoikountos, κατοικοῦντος) there — God himself. The temple is not abstract.
Swearing by heaven swears by God's throne and by him who sits on it. All oaths invoke God. Their hairsplitting is a fiction.

Fourth woe. Tithe mint, dill, cumin — minute precision. Neglect the weightier matters of the Torah: justice (krisis, κρίσις), mercy (eleos, ἔλεος), faithfulness (pistis, πίστις). Both should be done — but priorities matter.
Blind guides — strain out a gnat, swallow a camel. Hyperbolic illustration. Tiny scruples, massive blind spots.
Fifth woe. Clean the outside of cup and dish — inside full of greed (harpages, ἁρπαγῆς, robbery) and self-indulgence (akrasias, ἀκρασίας). Ritual purity masks exploitation.
Blind Pharisee — first clean the inside; then the outside will be clean. Interior is the cause; ritual exterior cannot remedy interior corruption.
Sixth woe. Whitewashed tombs — beautiful outside, dead bones and uncleanness inside. Passover whitewashing of tombs to prevent corpse-defilement is the local reference. The image is devastating.
Outside righteous, inside hypocrisy and lawlessness (anomia, ἀνομία). Same diagnosis from a different angle.
Seventh woe. Building tombs of the prophets, decorating monuments of the righteous. Honoring dead prophets they would have killed alive.
'If we had been in our fathers' days, we would not have joined them in shedding prophets' blood.' Self-flattering counterfactual. They imagine themselves above their ancestors' violence.
By saying so, you testify against yourselves — you are sons of those who murdered prophets. The protest is the confession. Lineage acknowledged in the very disowning.
'Fill up then the measure of your fathers.' Sarcasm: complete the family vocation. They will kill the climactic prophet within days.
'Snakes, brood of vipers — how will you escape being sentenced to Gehenna?' Echoes John's identical phrasing (3:7). Rhetorical question with terminal weight.

'I send you prophets, wise men, scribes — some you will kill and crucify, others flog in synagogues, persecute from town to town.' Future tense: the church's persecution is in view. Apostolic missions framed as continuation of prophetic line.

All righteous blood from Abel (Genesis 4) to Zechariah son of Berechiah (likely 2 Chronicles 24:20-22, with possible name conflation) — first martyr to last in Hebrew canon. The whole canon's bloodshed comes to a head in this generation.
'All this will come on this generation.' AD 70 — Jerusalem's destruction — fulfills it. The accumulated judgment falls within a generation.

'Jerusalem, Jerusalem' — twice, lament-vocative. 'How often I would have gathered your children as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing!' Maternal divine image rare and tender. The willingness deficit is on Jerusalem's side.
'Behold, your house is left to you desolate.' The temple — God's house — now 'your house.' Divine departure announced. Ichabod (1 Samuel 4:21) on the temple.

'You will not see me again until you say, Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord' (Psalm 118:26). Crowds said this at his entry (21:9); Jerusalem corporately must say it before re-encountering him. Eschatological recognition required.
